title: Mapungubwe flourishes
summary: A sophisticated southern African kingdom controls regional trade.
what: Mapungubwe developed elite political authority and traded gold and ivory across the Indian Ocean world.
why: It challenges colonial narratives that present complex state formation as a European import.
colle: Why are precolonial African states often absent from national narratives?

title: Dutch settlement at the Cape
summary: A permanent settler colony is established.
what: The Dutch East India Company created a refreshment station that expanded through land seizure, forced labour and slavery.
why: The settlement laid foundations for Afrikaner identity and racial hierarchy.
colle: How did labour and land shape racial categories?

title: The Great Trek
summary: Boer settlers move inland beyond British rule.
what: Thousands of Voortrekkers established republics after conflicts with African kingdoms and communities.
why: The Trek became a powerful Afrikaner nationalist myth of independence and divine mission.
colle: How do migration stories become nationalist myths?

title: Diamonds and gold
summary: Mineral discoveries transform the region.
what: Diamonds at Kimberley and gold on the Witwatersrand produced rapid industrialisation and migrant labour.
why: The mineral economy intensified imperial rivalry, urbanisation and racial labour control.
colle: Did mining create modern South Africa or entrench inequality?

title: The South African War
summary: Britain defeats the Boer republics.
what: The war included guerrilla fighting and British concentration camps for Boer and Black civilians.
why: Its settlement reconciled white elites while largely excluding the Black majority.
colle: Can reconciliation between former enemies be unjust to excluded groups?

title: The Union of South Africa
summary: A self-governing dominion unites four colonies.
what: Political power was consolidated in a white-controlled state within the British Empire.
why: Union created national institutions while excluding most Black South Africans from citizenship and power.
colle: Was the modern state founded on racial exclusion?

title: The ANC is founded
summary: A national organisation begins the struggle for African political rights.
what: The South African Native National Congress, later the ANC, sought to resist dispossession and exclusion.
why: It became the leading liberation movement and later the dominant governing party.
colle: Can a liberation movement successfully become a governing party?

title: The Natives Land Act
summary: Black land ownership is severely restricted.
what: The law confined most African land rights to small designated reserves.
why: It entrenched dispossession, rural poverty and the migrant-labour system long before formal apartheid.
colle: Should land reform prioritise restitution, redistribution or productivity?

title: Apartheid begins
summary: The National Party formalises racial separation.
what: A network of laws classified people by race and controlled residence, work, education, movement and political rights.
why: Apartheid converted existing segregation into a comprehensive authoritarian system.
colle: How did law make racial domination appear normal?

title: The Freedom Charter
summary: The anti-apartheid movement sets out a democratic alternative.
what: The Congress of the People declared that South Africa belongs to all who live in it.
why: Its principles influenced the later democratic constitution and continue to shape arguments about social and economic justice.
colle: Has post-apartheid South Africa fulfilled the Freedom Charter?

title: The Sharpeville massacre
summary: Police kill protesters opposing pass laws.
what: Sixty-nine people were killed, many shot in the back, during a peaceful demonstration.
why: The massacre prompted international outrage, the banning of liberation movements and a turn towards armed struggle.
colle: When does repression make armed resistance inevitable?

title: The Rivonia Trial
summary: Nelson Mandela and other leaders receive life sentences.
what: The defendants used the trial to condemn apartheid and explain the turn to sabotage.
why: Mandela’s imprisonment made him a global symbol of resistance.
colle: Can a political trial strengthen the cause it seeks to suppress?

title: The Soweto Uprising
summary: Schoolchildren revolt against apartheid education.
what: Police fired on students protesting compulsory Afrikaans instruction.
why: The uprising radicalised a generation and destroyed the regime’s claim to stability.
colle: Why are students often central to democratic movements?

title: Mandela is freed and the ANC unbanned
summary: Negotiated transition begins.
what: President F. W. de Klerk lifted bans on liberation movements and released Nelson Mandela.
why: The decision opened a difficult process combining negotiation, violence and constitutional compromise.
colle: Why did apartheid end through negotiation rather than revolution?

title: The first democratic election
summary: Universal suffrage creates the “Rainbow Nation”.
what: The ANC won the election and Nelson Mandela became president in a Government of National Unity.
why: The peaceful transfer of power became a global symbol of democratic possibility.
colle: Was the negotiated transition too generous to the old elite?

title: The Constitution and the TRC
summary: A rights-based constitution and public reckoning shape the new democracy.
what: The constitution entrenched equality and socio-economic rights, while the Truth and Reconciliation Commission heard testimony about apartheid crimes.
why: South Africa became a model of constitutionalism and restorative justice, though material inequality endured.
colle: Can truth and reconciliation substitute for punishment and redistribution?

title: The Mbeki presidency
summary: The post-Mandela era prioritises African renewal and economic growth.
what: Thabo Mbeki promoted the African Renaissance and market-oriented policy but his government’s HIV/AIDS denialism caused immense harm.
why: The period illustrates the tension between technocratic ambition and democratic accountability.
colle: How should democracies respond when leaders reject scientific evidence?

title: The FIFA World Cup
summary: South Africa hosts a global sporting event.
what: The first World Cup held in Africa showcased infrastructure, culture and national pride.
why: The event strengthened soft power but also raised questions about public spending and lasting benefits.
colle: Do mega-events produce lasting social benefits?

title: The Marikana massacre
summary: Police kill striking mineworkers.
what: Thirty-four workers were shot during a labour dispute at a platinum mine.
why: Marikana exposed the persistence of economic inequality, labour conflict and state violence after apartheid.
colle: Why has political freedom not ended economic exploitation?

title: Rhodes Must Fall and Fees Must Fall
summary: Students demand decolonisation and affordable education.
what: Campus movements challenged colonial symbols, curricula, fees and institutional racism.
why: They placed unfinished transformation and generational frustration at the centre of national debate.
colle: What would a genuinely decolonised university look like?

title: Zuma resigns
summary: A presidency defined by “state capture” ends.
what: Jacob Zuma left office under pressure from the ANC after years of corruption allegations and institutional damage.
why: The transition to Cyril Ramaphosa promised renewal but revealed the difficulty of reforming a dominant party from within.
colle: Can a dominant party effectively police its own corruption?

title: July unrest
summary: Violence and looting expose social fragility.
what: Protests after Zuma’s imprisonment escalated into widespread unrest, particularly in KwaZulu-Natal and Gauteng.
why: The crisis revealed inequality, unemployment, weak institutions and political factionalism.
colle: How does extreme inequality threaten democracy?

title: The ANC loses its majority and a GNU is formed
summary: One-party dominance ends after thirty years.
what: The ANC won about 40% of the vote, Cyril Ramaphosa was re-elected by Parliament and a multi-party Government of National Unity was formed.
why: The shift created an unprecedented test of coalition politics, accountability and policy compromise.
colle: Can coalition government renew South African democracy?
